task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
The blinking red light atop the crude, thorn-covered cage resolved into the lens of a small camera. From its hidden perch in the gnarled branches above, a wire snaked away into the dense undergrowth. In the trap, Cody pushed himself up, dusting off his hands. The baitmouse, now frantically ringing its bell beside him, squeaked in terror.

“Quiet, little fella,” Cody whispered, his eyes fixed on the camera. “It’s gonna be okay.”

But his heart hammered against his ribs. He knew that camera. Everyone in the outback knew the stories of the poacher Percival C. McLeach and his strange, mechanized traps.

* * *

Several miles away, in the rusted, cavernous hull of a repurposed mining dredge, a monitor flickered to life. The greenish glow illuminated a face etched with greed and a permanent, unpleasant smirk. Percival C. McLeach leaned forward, his eyes narrowing at the image of the boy in the trap.

“Well, well, well,” he drawled, his voice like grinding stones. “What do we have here? A little bush rat in my snare.”

Slithering across the cluttered control panel, a sleek, sharp-eyed goanna named Joanna hissed, her forked tongue tasting the air. “Is it tasty? Can I bite it?”

“Not yet, Joanna,” McLeach said, a plan coiling in his mind like a snake. He’d seen that boy before, zipping through the bush with every creature from a kookaburra to a wallaby at his heels. A kid like that… a kid who talked to animals… might just know exactly where a certain hard-to-find golden eagle was nesting. He stroked the wanted poster pinned next to the monitor, his own face staring back at him under the word ‘POACHER’. Marahute was the last prize, the one that would set him up for life. And now, fate had just delivered her biggest weakness right into his lap.

“Time to go collect our bait,” he chuckled, grabbing a heavy, coiled rope and his electric prod. “The real catch’ll come swoopin’ in after.”

* * *

Back at the trap, Cody was working his knife against a thick vine binding the cage door. “Almost… got it…” he grunted. The little mouse had ceased its panic and was now helping by gnawing on a thinner strand.

Suddenly, the forest fell silent. The birdsong ceased. The only sound was the ominous *crunch-crunch* of heavy boots approaching through the dry brush. A shadow, long and distorted, fell across the cage.

Cody froze, looking up. Towering over him was McLeach, his silhouette blocking the sun. The poacher wore a stained duster coat, and his smile showed several missing teeth.

“G’day, mate,” McLeach said, his voice deceptively casual. “Find yourself in a bit of a pickle?”

“Let me out,” Cody demanded, trying to sound braver than he felt. He subtly closed his hand over the golden eagle feather tucked into his belt.

“All in good time,” McLeach said, kneeling. His eyes, cold and calculating, scanned Cody. They lingered on the feather. “That’s a pretty trinket. Rare. Where’d a nipper like you get somethin’ like that?”

“Found it,” Cody said quickly.

Joanna’s head popped up from McLeach’s collar. “He’s lying! I can smell the eagle on him! Big, shiny, tasty eagle!”

McLeach’s fake friendliness vanished. “I think you and me need to have a little chat.” With a brutal yank, he pulled the cage door open, vines snapping. Before Cody could react, a powerful hand clamped around his arm and hauled him out. The baitmouse scurried into a hole, bell jingling faintly.

“Let me go!” Cody yelled, struggling. He thought of Faloo, of the little wombats, of Marahute sitting on her warm eggs. He couldn’t lead this monster to them.

McLeach effortlessly lifted Cody, carrying him under one arm like a sack of potatoes. “Now, now, no fuss. You’re gonna help an old bloke out. See, I’m a collector. And there’s one magnificent bird I’ve had my eye on. Big, gold, lays whopper eggs. You know the one.”

“I don’t know anything!” Cody shouted, kicking.

McLeach stopped and turned Cody to face him, his grip tightening. “Don’t play games with me, boy. I saw you on the cliff. I saw you cut her free.” He leaned in, his breath sour. “You’re gonna take me to her nest. Or things are gonna get very unpleasant for every furry and feathered ‘friend’ you’ve got in this valley. Starting with that chatty kookaburra who’s been followin’ us.”

Cody’s blood ran cold. He’d heard Faloo’s distant, worried call just minutes ago. McLeach had heard it too.

The poacher threw Cody over the saddle of a waiting, grimy dirt bike and secured his hands with rough rope. “Think about it on the ride back,” McLeach said, kicking the engine to life with a deafening roar. “The mighty Marahute for the life of one scrawny kid. I reckon she’d make that trade. She’s soft, like you.”

As the bike lurched forward, tearing through the pristine bush, Cody’s mind raced. He couldn’t show McLeach the nest. But he also couldn’t let his friends get hurt. He looked back at the forest, shrinking behind them. Somewhere in the canopy, Faloo would be watching, sounding the alarm. The animals would know he was taken.

He had to be smart. He had to be brave. Like his dad would have been. The feather in his belt seemed to burn against his skin, a secret promise. He made a silent vow to Marahute, to her unhatched chicks: he wouldn’t break. He would find a way.

The dredge loomed ahead, a monstrous scar on the landscape. Its mechanical jaws hung open like a hungry mouth. McLeach drove straight into its dark belly, the sound of the engine echoing in the metal cavern. He cut the engine, and the sudden silence was more terrifying than the noise.

He pulled Cody off the bike and shoved him toward a rusty cage suspended from the ceiling. “Home sweet home,” McLeach grunted, unlocking it. “You’ve got ‘til sundown to decide to be helpful. After that…” He gestured to Joanna, who was eyeing Cody with culinary interest. “Well, I get creative.”

The cage door clanged shut, the lock clicking with finality. McLeach walked to his control panel, flipping switches. On a bank of monitors, Cody saw live feeds from dozens of cameras hidden throughout the preserve. He saw a family of kangaroos grazing, a platypus in a stream, and… his heart leapt… a familiar, frantic kookaburra flying from tree to tree near the trap site.

Faloo was looking for him.

Cody curled his fingers around the bars, a plan beginning to form. He was small, he was quick, and he wasn’t alone. The animals were his army. All he had to do was get a message out. As McLeach busied himself with his cruel gadgets, Cody’s eyes scanned the dredge’s interior, looking for weakness, for a way to turn the poacher’s own lair against him. The great golden eagle had trusted him with her life, and now her freedom depended on his cunning. The adventure had just taken a dark turn, but Cody was far from defeated.

 Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter .

response:
In the ongoing national conversation about racial justice and police conduct, the voices of celebrities often amplify issues that might otherwise go unnoticed. When Taraji P. Henson, the acclaimed actress known for her powerful roles in films like "Hidden Figures" and the television series "Empire," speaks out, the public listens. Recently, Henson brought a deeply personal matter to light, revealing that her son, Marcel, was subjected to what she describes as racial profiling by police officers. This incident, which occurred at the University of Southern California (USC), has sparked outrage, drawn attention to the experiences of students of color on campus, and prompted promises of independent investigation. It underscores a pervasive issue that extends far beyond the gates of prestigious universities, touching the very heart of systemic bias in law enforcement.

According to Henson’s account, the unsettling event took place on a seemingly ordinary afternoon near the USC campus. Marcel, a student at the university, was reportedly stopped by police officers while walking through the neighborhood. The details remain somewhat vague, but Henson emphasized that her son was not engaged in any suspicious activity; he was simply going about his day. The stop, she claims, was unwarranted and based solely on his appearance as a young Black man. “It’s every mother’s nightmare,” Henson stated in an emotional interview. “To know that your child is being targeted because of the color of his skin, in a place where he should feel safe and focused on his education, is heartbreaking.” This incident is not isolated in the context of USC, an institution that has grappled with tensions surrounding campus safety and racial equity in the past.

Taraji P. Henson, who has been an outspoken advocate for mental health awareness and racial equality, did not mince words in her response. She took to social media and various media outlets to share her family’s experience, framing it within the larger struggle against racial profiling. “We cannot stay silent,” she declared. “This is about my son today, but it’s about so many other sons and daughters every day.” Henson’s platform gives her a unique ability to shed light on these injustices, and she has used it to call for accountability and change. Her pain is palpable, but it is matched by a determined resolve to ensure that such episodes are not swept under the rug. She has urged USC and local law enforcement to examine their protocols and implicit biases that lead to disproportionate stops of Black and brown individuals.

In a revealing twist, the head of USC’s Department of Public Safety, John Thomas, responded to the allegations by sharing his own personal encounters with racial profiling. Thomas, who oversees campus security, acknowledged the gravity of Henson’s claims and expressed empathy born from similar experiences. “As a Black man in America, I have faced racial profiling myself,” Thomas said in a public statement. “It is a dehumanizing reality that no one should endure, especially not our students.” His admission adds a layer of complexity to the situation, highlighting that even those in positions of authority within security apparatuses are not immune to the biases they are tasked with combating. Thomas’s statement suggests an institutional awareness of the problem, but it also raises questions about what concrete steps have been taken to eradicate such practices within the campus police force.

Amidst the responses, John Thomas has vowed to conduct a thorough, independent investigation into the specifics of the stop involving Marcel. “We are committed to transparency and justice,” Thomas asserted. “This incident will be scrutinized with the utmost care, and we will take appropriate action based on the findings.” The investigation is expected to review body camera footage, interview the officers involved, and assess whether proper procedures were followed. However, given Thomas’s dual role as investigator and a figure who has admitted to personal bias, some community members have called for an entirely external review to ensure impartiality. The outcome of this probe could set a precedent for how USC handles similar complaints in the future and whether substantive policy changes will be implemented.

The episode at USC is a microcosm of a national crisis. Racial profiling by law enforcement remains a contentious and deeply rooted issue across the United States. Data from various studies consistently show that Black individuals are disproportionately stopped, searched, and subjected to use of force compared to their white counterparts. On college campuses, where young adults are navigating independence and academic pressure, such encounters can have traumatic effects, undermining their sense of belonging and safety. Universities like USC, with diverse student bodies, face the challenge of balancing security with inclusivity. Incidents like the one involving Marcel Henson revive debates about the role of police in educational settings and the need for alternative, community-based safety measures that do not rely on punitive approaches.

Looking beyond USC, the story resonates with a history of advocacy and resistance against racial injustice. From the Black Lives Matter movement to local grassroots organizations, there is a sustained push for police reform and accountability. Taraji P. Henson’s decision to speak out aligns with a broader pattern of public figures using their influence to champion social causes. It also serves as a reminder that racial profiling is not confined to any one region or institution; it is a systemic ailment that requires systemic solutions. As the investigation unfolds, it will be crucial to monitor whether it leads to meaningful change or becomes another footnote in a long list of unresolved grievances. The hope among many is that this incident will catalyze a deeper examination of biases and practices, not just at USC but within law enforcement agencies nationwide.

In conclusion, the racial profiling allegation involving Taraji P. Henson’s son at USC is a poignant illustration of the enduring challenges faced by communities of color. It highlights the personal toll of systemic bias and the courage required to confront it. With USC’s Public Safety boss acknowledging his own experiences and pledging an investigation, there is a glimmer of potential for accountability. However, true progress will depend on whether this moment translates into sustained action—revised training programs, community oversight, and a commitment to dismantling the structures that permit racial profiling. As Henson and others continue to advocate, the public must listen, learn, and demand a future where safety and dignity are not contingent on skin color. The path forward is fraught, but with unwavering attention and effort, justice can be more than an aspiration; it can be a reality for all.
